title: Advanced Analytical Model for Interpreting Oscillatory Pumping Tests With Wellbore Skin and Rate-Dependent Skin Effects

research.question: How can an oscillatory pumping-test solution represent wellbore storage, wellbore skin, and rate-dependent skin so that aquifer parameters can be estimated from non-ideal field heads?
research.system: A radially symmetric oscillatory pumping test in a confined, homogeneous, isotropic aquifer with a fully penetrating well, wellbore storage, wellbore skin, and rate-dependent head loss at the screen.
research.inputData: A variable sinusoidal pumping rate, aquifer and well parameters, and water-level observations from a previously reported Boise Hydrogeophysical Research Site oscillatory pumping test.
research.method: Form the radial flow boundary-value problem, add well-storage and rate-dependent skin conditions, derive the direct-Fourier analytical response, analyze parameter sensitivity, estimate field parameters, and compare three skin formulations.
research.benchmark: Field heads and the SEE and ME fit statistics are compared across the full solution, a solution with the rate-dependent factor set to zero, and a solution omitting both skin effects.
research.uncertainty: The interpretation is bounded by the homogeneous-aquifer assumption, parameter correlation, near-well sensitivity of the skin factors, and differences between observed and predicted heads summarized by SEE and ME.
research.conclusion: Including both wellbore skin and rate-dependent skin reproduces the sharp sawtooth-like field response more closely than the two reduced formulations, while estimated parameters represent mean conditions within the test radius of influence under the homogeneity assumption.

narrative.throughline: Follow one sinusoidal pumping signal through the well screen and aquifer to see how rate-dependent head loss reshapes the response used for parameter estimation.
narrative.audienceStartingPoint: A pumping test links an imposed well discharge to a measured hydraulic-head response.
narrative.stakes: If screen-scale head losses are assigned to the aquifer instead, fitted transmissivity and storage can be biased.
narrative.resolution: The complete boundary response makes the field sawtooth peaks visible in the model while preserving the homogeneous-aquifer boundary on inferred parameters.

background.premise: OPTs alternate extraction and injection with no net water extraction or injection over a complete cycle.
background.visibleEvidence: A well alternates extraction and injection around a zero-net-volume baseline.
background.whyNow: The reversible pumping cycle establishes why an oscillatory test is scientifically useful before its non-ideal well response is examined.
background.boundary: This premise retains only the source's zero-net-water advantage and does not imply zero head loss or zero solute movement.
background.sourceExcerpt: Compared to constant-rate pumping tests, OPTs offer several advantages, including no net water
background.visualObject: Confined aquifer cross-section with an oscillating well flux and zero-net-volume gauge
background.motionPurpose: Make the alternating test cycle and its net-zero abstraction visually concrete.
background.stepDetail: Trace one extraction half-cycle; reverse the well arrows for injection; return the cumulative-volume marker to zero.
background.transitionBridge: Keep the oscillating well visible and reveal the altered material immediately around its screen.

background.premise: Wellbore skin is a near-well region with different permeability than the surrounding formation.
background.visibleEvidence: A thin annulus around the well contrasts with the ambient aquifer and changes the local head gradient.
background.whyNow: The physical skin region must be visible before its head-loss factor enters a boundary condition.
background.boundary: The premise concerns formation alteration around the wellbore rather than rate dependence at the screen.
background.locator: PDF page 2, Introduction, first paragraph; Figure 1
background.sourceExcerpt: The wellbore skin is a small region surrounding a wellbore that has different permeability than the surrounding formation.
background.visualObject: Pumping-well cross-section with a distinct skin annulus embedded in the aquifer
background.motionPurpose: Localize conventional skin loss to a physical region around the well.
background.stepDetail: Reveal the screen; grow a narrow annulus around it; contrast the head gradient across the annulus with the ambient formation.
background.transitionBridge: Move attention from the annular formation to high-velocity flow through the screen itself.

background.premise: Rate-dependent skin is a screen-scale head loss proportional to the pumping rate under non-Darcian flow.
background.visibleEvidence: Velocity arrows crowd through the screen as a head-loss bracket grows with the pumping-rate magnitude.
background.whyNow: This second loss mechanism explains why flow reversal can reshape an oscillatory head response.
background.boundary: The rate-dependent skin is assigned at the well screen while flow farther from the well remains Darcian.
background.sourceExcerpt: non-Darcian flow due to pumping at the well screen results in head loss, known as the rate-dependent skin, that is proportional to the pumping rate
background.visualObject: Well-screen velocity field with a pumping-rate-linked head-loss bracket
background.motionPurpose: Tie the extra head loss to screen velocity and pumping-rate magnitude.
background.stepDetail: Increase the screen-flow arrows; extend the head-loss bracket in proportion; reverse the arrows at the half-cycle boundary.
background.transitionBridge: Place the conventional skin annulus and rate-dependent screen loss on the same test geometry.

background.premise: No existing OPT model jointly incorporates wellbore skin and rate-dependent skin.
background.visibleEvidence: Two separate loss objects sit beside an incomplete response model that accepts only one of them.
background.whyNow: The missing joint treatment defines the analytical problem solved by the paper.
background.boundary: The gap concerns the combined skin effects in OPT models with variable pumping rate.
background.sourceExcerpt: existing OPT models do not incorporate the joint effects of wellbore skin and rate-dependent skin.
background.visualObject: Oscillatory response model with separate wellbore-skin and rate-dependent-skin inputs
background.motionPurpose: Expose the missing model connection between the two visible loss mechanisms.
background.stepDetail: Bring both loss objects toward the model boundary; leave one connection absent; hold the resulting response mismatch on screen.
background.transitionBridge: Rebuild the model from the radial aquifer equation outward to the well boundary.

methods.input: Confined aquifer geometry with variable pumping rate and radial hydraulic head.
methods.output: Aquifer head equation for radially symmetric flow produced by the OPT.
methods.validity: Darcy's Law and the continuity equation describe the radial aquifer flow under the stated confined homogeneous isotropic assumptions.
methods.sourceExcerpt: the aquifer head for radially symmetric flow produced by the OPT can be described by Cardiff and Sayler (2016):
methods.visualObject: Radial aquifer rings carrying an oscillatory head field away from the pumping well
methods.motionPurpose: Turn the physical cross-section into the radial field governed by the aquifer equation.
methods.stepDetail: Center the coordinate system on the well; trace radial head gradients; attach storage change to the expanding rings; reveal Equation 1 from those objects.
methods.transitionBridge: Carry the radial head field to the well surface where stored water modifies the imposed flux.

methods.input: Radial aquifer head equation with a pumping well that stores water at the well surface.
methods.output: Well surface boundary condition coupling aquifer flux to pumping rate and well storage.
methods.validity: The well-surface relation uses the Papadopulos and Cooper representation for wellbore storage.
methods.sourceExcerpt: The boundary condition at the well surface considering the effect of wellbore storage
methods.visualObject: Well column storage marker coupled to radial flux at the screen
methods.motionPurpose: Show that imposed pumping is divided between aquifer flow and changing water stored in the well.
methods.stepDetail: Hold the radial field at the screen; raise and lower the well-column marker; split the imposed flux into aquifer and storage contributions; reveal Equation 3.
methods.transitionBridge: Retain the well-surface relation and add the rate-linked screen loss beside the conventional skin term.

methods.input: Well surface relation with conventional skin and an oscillating screen flow rate.
methods.output: Inner boundary condition containing the rate-dependent screen head loss.
methods.validity: The additional condition represents rate-dependent skin effects through the factor multiplying the wellbore flow rate.
methods.sourceExcerpt: To include the rate-dependent skin effects, an additional condition is required (Lin & Yeh, 2020a):
methods.visualObject: Well boundary with a skin drop and a rate-linked screen-loss bracket
methods.motionPurpose: Insert the missing rate-dependent loss directly at the inner boundary.
methods.stepDetail: Keep the well and aquifer heads separated; place the conventional skin drop at the interface; attach the rate-linked bracket to screen flow; reveal Equation 4.
methods.transitionBridge: Carry the completed boundary-value problem into a representation matched to sinusoidal forcing.

methods.input: Boundary value problem for sinusoidal pumping with both skin effects.
methods.output: Wave phasor equation with transformed well and aquifer boundary relations.
methods.validity: The closed-form solution is derived by the direct Fourier method for sinusoidal pumping.
methods.sourceExcerpt: A closed-form solution to the boundary value problem described in Section 2.1 is derived by means of the direct Fourier method.
methods.visualObject: Sinusoidal head traces collapsing into aquifer and well phasor arrows
methods.motionPurpose: Replace repeating time traces with phasors that preserve amplitude and phase information.
methods.stepDetail: Trace one full head cycle; wrap the trace around a rotating reference; retain the aquifer and well phasors; reveal the transformed relations.
methods.transitionBridge: Place the two transformed inner-boundary equations side by side for elimination.

methods.output: Single inner boundary relation in Equations 8 and 9.
methods.validity: Eliminating the wellbore phasor between Equations 7a and 7b leaves one condition for the aquifer phasor.
methods.sourceExcerpt: By combining Equations 7a and 7b to eliminate
methods.visualObject: Two boundary-equation lanes sharing a wellbore phasor term that disappears
methods.motionPurpose: Make elimination of the internal well variable visible before the response is solved.
methods.stepDetail: Align the shared wellbore-phasor terms; cancel the matched pair; collect the surviving aquifer terms into the revised boundary relation.
methods.transitionBridge: Attach the revised inner relation and far-field condition to the transformed radial equation.

methods.input: Equation 6 with the revised inner boundary relation and unaffected far field.
methods.validity: The radial solution uses zero-order and first-order modified Bessel functions of the second kind with diffusivity defined by transmissivity and storage.
methods.sourceExcerpt: Thus, the solution of Equation 6 takes the form:
methods.visualObject: Boundary-constrained radial phasor field expressed by Bessel response terms
methods.motionPurpose: Resolve the transformed radial equation under the revised inner and far-field conditions.
methods.stepDetail: Solve the transformed radial equation for its boundary-constrained phasor.
methods.transitionBridge: Project the solved phasor into observable time in M07.

methods.input: The solved aquifer phasor from Equation 10.
methods.output: Time-domain aquifer head response in Equation 11.
methods.validity: The direct Fourier representation recovers the sinusoidal head by taking the imaginary part of the phasor response.
methods.sourceExcerpt: Consequently, the head solution is expressed as
methods.visualObject: Solved radial phasor rotating into a sinusoidal hydraulic-head trace
methods.motionPurpose: Return the transformed solution to the observable head domain.
methods.stepDetail: Project the imaginary phasor component onto a time-domain head trace.
methods.transitionBridge: Use the projected head as the model output perturbed in M08.

methods.input: Solved model output with aquifer and well input parameters.
methods.output: Signed model-output sensitivities for individual aquifer and well parameters.
methods.validity: The sensitivity analysis coefficient relates a small parameter change to the corresponding change in the wave-phasor model output.
methods.sourceExcerpt: A sensitivity analysis was performed to identify the effects of the aquifer parameters on the model output
methods.visualObject: Parameter perturbation sliders linked to signed changes in the head-response phasor
methods.motionPurpose: Expose how each parameter changes the modeled response before parameters are ranked.
methods.stepDetail: Perturb one parameter around its reference state; hold the remaining parameters fixed; show the signed phasor change; normalize the coefficient.
methods.transitionBridge: Gather the individual sensitivity magnitudes into one relative-importance measure in M09.

methods.input: Normalized sensitivity magnitudes for all model parameters.
methods.output: DIM share for each parameter's sensitivity within the total sensitivity.
methods.validity: DIM quantifies a parameter's sensitivity contribution relative to the sensitivities of all parameters for the phasor response.
methods.locator: PDF page 4, Section 2.3, Equation 13; PDF pages 7-8, Figure 6
methods.sourceExcerpt: Equation 13 quantifies the contribution of a parameter's sensitivity to the sensitivities of all parameters in response to a phasor change. Thus, a higher value of DIM means a higher sensitivity of the given parameter.
methods.visualObject: Sensitivity contributions partitioning a unit DIM bar beside the oscillatory response
methods.motionPurpose: Convert separate sensitivities into a common relative-importance scale.
methods.stepDetail: Place all sensitivity magnitudes on one baseline; divide each by their total; fill the corresponding share of the DIM bar; retain the time-varying response context.
methods.transitionBridge: Map the positive fitting parameters to logarithmic coordinates in M10.

methods.input: Positive transmissivity storage skin-factor and rate-dependent-factor parameters.
methods.operation: Transform T S Sf D logarithmically
methods.output: Logarithmic coordinates for all four estimated parameters.
methods.validity: The paper applies the same logarithmic transformation to T S Sf and D to prevent negative estimates.
methods.locator: PDF page 5, Section 2.4, paragraph after Equation 14
methods.sourceExcerpt: A logarithmic transformation was applied to T, S, Sf, and D to avoid generating a negative esti-
methods.visualObject: Four positive parameter axes folding onto logarithmic coordinates
methods.motionPurpose: Keep every fitted aquifer and skin parameter in its positive physical domain.
methods.stepDetail: Map T S Sf and D from positive values to their logarithmic coordinates.

methods.input: Digitized field head observations with the Equation 11 response model.
methods.output: Candidate Equation 11 parameter values for post-processing.
methods.validity: The source identifies differential evolution as the first method in the combined parameter-estimation approach.
methods.locator: PDF page 5, Section 2.4, paragraph after Equation 14
methods.sourceExcerpt: employing a combined approach of the differential evolution method
methods.visualObject: Differential-evolution candidates moving over the squared-error surface
methods.motionPurpose: Generate candidate parameter values from the field-head objective.
methods.stepDetail: Estimate candidate parameters with differential evolution.
methods.transitionBridge: Pass the candidates to Levenberg-Marquardt post-processing in M12.

methods.input: Differential-evolution parameter candidates and the squared-error field objective.
methods.output: Post-processed parameter values and predicted field heads for each skin formulation.
methods.validity: The cited Levenberg-Marquardt method is explicitly assigned to the post-processing phase of parameter estimation.
methods.locator: PDF page 5, Section 2.4, paragraph after Equation 14
methods.sourceExcerpt: the Levenberg-Marquardt method (Gill et al., 1981) in the post-processing phase
methods.visualObject: Candidate marker settling onto the field-head squared-error minimum
methods.motionPurpose: Refine the candidate parameters against observed oscillatory heads.
methods.stepDetail: Fit the post-processing refinement from the differential-evolution candidates.
methods.transitionBridge: Calculate standard error of estimation for the fitted heads in M13.

methods.input: Observed and predicted field heads with the stated degrees of freedom.
methods.output: Standard error of estimation for each fitted solution.
methods.validity: Equation 15a defines SEE as the square root of mean squared prediction error adjusted by the degrees of freedom.
methods.visualObject: Squared field-head errors accumulating into an SEE gauge
methods.motionPurpose: Measure fitted dispersion separately from signed bias.
methods.stepDetail: Square the prediction errors and scale their sum by the degrees of freedom.
methods.transitionBridge: Calculate signed mean error in M14.

methods.input: Signed prediction errors between observed and modeled field heads.
methods.output: Mean prediction error for each fitted solution.
methods.validity: Equation 15b averages the signed prediction errors and therefore retains systematic bias.
methods.visualObject: Signed field-head errors balancing on an ME gauge
methods.motionPurpose: Expose systematic prediction bias separately from SEE.
methods.stepDetail: Average the signed prediction errors for each fitted solution.
methods.transitionBridge: Compare the three skin formulations with both metrics in M15.

methods.input: Field predictions from Solution 1 and the two reduced skin solutions.
methods.output: Relative peak agreement with SEE and ME evidence for the three solutions.
methods.validity: The same field observations are evaluated with three versions of Equation 11 and goodness of fit is summarized by SEE and ME.
methods.sourceExcerpt: Solution 1 emerged as the superior model, yielding lower SEE and ME values than the other solutions.
methods.visualObject: Three predicted head traces over the measured field peaks with paired fit-error markers
methods.motionPurpose: Make the consequence of retaining both skin mechanisms visible at the sharp field peaks.
methods.stepDetail: Lock the measured hydrograph in place; overlay each fitted solution; magnify the peak region; place the reported fit statistics beside their owning traces.
methods.transitionBridge: Return the best-matching response to the original well and aquifer with its interpretation boundary.

symbols.transition: The alternating arrows become the forcing at the well boundary.

symbols.meaning: hydraulic head in the aquifer at radial distance r and time t
symbols.transition: The radial field receives its function label before Equation 1 appears.

symbols.visualObject: water level in the pumping well
symbols.transition: The well-column level becomes the well-head term in the storage boundary.
A color-matched label moves from the radial flux path into the flow equation.

symbols.visualObject: aquifer storage change attached to radial rings
symbols.transition: A brace on the changing stored volume becomes the storage term.

symbols.visualObject: head drop across the wellbore skin annulus
symbols.transition: The annular head-drop bracket receives the skin-factor label.

symbols.visualObject: rate-linked head-loss bracket at the screen
symbols.transition: The screen-loss bracket becomes the coefficient paired with pumping rate.

symbols.visualObject: signed phasor change from the kth parameter perturbation
symbols.meaning: normalized sensitivity of model output to the kth input parameter
symbols.transition: The signed response displacement receives the sensitivity label.

symbols.visualObject: kth share of the unit sensitivity bar
symbols.meaning: differential importance measure for the kth parameter
symbols.transition: Each normalized sensitivity magnitude fills its share of the unit bar.

return.visualObject: the original oscillatory pumping-well cross-section beside measured field heads and the full-solution peak response
return.claim: The solution containing both wellbore skin and rate-dependent skin captures the sharp sawtooth-like field peaks that the reduced solutions underestimate.
return.claimBoundary: Under the model's aquifer-homogeneity assumption, inferred aquifer parameters represent mean values within the OPT radius of influence rather than resolved heterogeneous fields.
return.locator: PDF pages 9-10, Section 3.2 and Conclusions; Figure 7
return.sourceExcerpt: wellbore skin and rate-dependent skin is not only flexible in parameter estimation but also capable of capturing the unique sawtooth pattern (very high and sharp peaks) observed in the field-measured data, while the other two solutions underestimate the peak values. This explains the higher errors for Solutions 1 and 2 (see ME, Table 1). In addition to generating the primary peaks, the new solution generates additional peaks as a consequence of the inertial forces caused by the change in pumping direction. Although subtle, additional positive and negative peaks are also evident near the main peaks in the field data. The present solution is based on assumptions of aquifer homogeneity. Therefore, the estimated parameters reflect the mean value within a radius of influence of the OPT

visual.primaryObject: confined-aquifer cross-section with a fully penetrating oscillatory pumping well
visual.accentObject: rate-linked screen head-loss bracket that sharpens the modeled field response
visual.measuredQuantity: hydraulic head oscillation in the pumping well and aquifer
visual.geometryNotes: Keep the well, skin annulus, screen-flow arrows, radial aquifer rings, phasor response, sensitivity shares, and measured field peaks as continuous scientific objects across the sequence.
